text: Once utility incision was made and the camera was inserted, we were surprised to find that the fissures were completely fused.
In the fissureless technique, we first dissect the hilar structures and then move on to the fissure.
The hilar structures, namely the artery, the vein and the bronchus are divided and then the fissure is divided using endo staplers.
The main advantage of this technique lies in the fact that the fissures are completely avoided and any intrafiscial dissection is avoided.
This in turn avoids any prolonged air leak especially in cases where the fissure is densely fused.
As with any other conventional surgery, we start off this surgery by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament and the accompanying station 9 lymph node.
The fissureless technique however has a few disadvantages.

We cannot access the N1 level of lymph nodes as these are the interlobar nodes which are placed in between the lobes and sometimes we cannot make out certain variations in the arteries which can only be done by dissecting into the fissure.
So some surgeons mostly robotic and open surgeons still prefer to do the fissure first technique.
We then move on to the anterior part of the hilum where a pleural cut is made.
The station 10 lymph nodes in the anterior part are removed and the hilar structures are visualized.
This pleural cut is extended posteriorly as well.
The posterior hilar nodes are also removed and the surrounding soft tissues are cleared to clearly visualize the hilar structures in their entirety.
It is important to completely bare all the hilar structures in this technique.

All the soft tissues and the fibrous tissues surrounding the artery vein and the bronchus need to be completely dissected off as there is limited space for staplers to pass through at the hilum and hence there is very little room for error while performing a lobectomy by this technique.
Once the hilar structures are dissected completely, we will take a minute to review the anatomy.
On your screen, you can see two arrows pointing downwards.
The blue arrow points to the pulmonary vein and the red arrow to the pulmonary artery.
And in between these two structures is the main bronchus.
It is important to create adequate space between these structures for the staplers to pass through easily.
Now you can see the anterior view.
There are two arrows pointing upwards.
The blue arrow point to the vein and the green arrow to the bronchus.
Now a critical step of the surgery is being performed.
Enough space is being created between the artery and the bronchus.
And then we first divide the inferior pulmonary vein using the stapler.

In this procedure, the bronchus needs to be divided before dividing the artery and hence it is critical that the surgeon creates enough space between the bronchus and the artery before passing a stapler across as there is a high chance of injury to the pulmonary artery while dividing the bronchus.
Before final division of the bronchus, it is crucial to check whether we have clamped the right bronchus.
We ask the anesthetist to inflate the lung and check whether the upper lobe is expanding adequately before we go on to divide the bronchus.
We now move on to the arterial part of the dissection.
As explained earlier, it's important to completely bare the pulmonary artery.
So all the soft tissue, nodes, and the fat at the hilum is being completely cleared.
And following this, you can see a right angle dissector being passed across the pulmonary artery to create space for a stapler to pass through.

In the uniportal technique, this step is quite challenging, especially for the camera person as all the instruments and the camera is placed in an anterior port and pulmonary artery is a posteriorly based structure.
So to dissect the artery completely at the hilum can be quite challenging.
However, it can be accomplished.

There are certain drawbacks to this technique as in this technique we have to divide the vein first which may result in congestion of the lobe and more bleeding from the lung while handling it and in the uniportal technique because the angles are so limited passing staplers while dividing the hyaluronate structures is quite challenging.
However once we do this we move on and divide the fissure
It is important to demarket the fissure accurately as sometimes we can end up dividing the upper lobe branches while dividing and creating the fissure in this technique.
A useful technique here is to ask the anesthetist to inflate the lung.
As the bronchus is already divided, only the upper lobe will inflate and the lower lobe can be clearly demarketed and separated from the upper lobe.
The fissureless technique is a must-know for every thoracoscopic surgeon as it is effective in not only reducing air leaks but also in handling cases where there are dense fissures.
Post-operatively, this patient had an uneventful recovery.
